cross_connections.display_label: First cousins — both Leete granddaughters who married Hooker men.

narrative_blocks.header: Granddaughter of Governor William Leete
narrative_blocks.body: Her father was William Leete Jr., son of Governor William Leete — the only man to govern both New Haven Colony and Colony of Connecticut. Her first cousin Mercy Leete (daughter of her uncle Andrew) also married a Hooker man. Two of the Governor's granddaughters, through different sons, both married into the Hooker family.

narrative_blocks.header: Granddaughter of a Connecticut governor
narrative_blocks.body: Mary Leete was a granddaughter of Gov. William Leete, who governed both the New Haven and Connecticut colonies. She married Judge James Hooker in 1691 and they settled at Guilford.

burial.plot_notes: Cenotaph at Alder Brook Cemetery, Guilford; actual grave uncertain. SOURCE: FindAGrave.

cross_connections.display_label: First cousins — both Leete granddaughters who married Hooker men.

narrative_blocks.header: Granddaughter of Governor William Leete
narrative_blocks.body: Her father was William Leete Jr., son of Governor William Leete — the only man to govern both New Haven Colony and Colony of Connecticut. Her first cousin Mercy Leete (daughter of her uncle Andrew) also married a Hooker man. Two of the Governor's granddaughters, through different sons, both married into the Hooker family.

narrative_blocks.header: Granddaughter of a Connecticut governor
narrative_blocks.body: Mary Leete was a granddaughter of Gov. William Leete, who governed both the New Haven and Connecticut colonies. She married Judge James Hooker in 1691 and they settled at Guilford.

burial.plot_notes: Cenotaph at Alder Brook Cemetery, Guilford; actual grave uncertain. SOURCE: FindAGrave.

cross_connections.display_label: Governor of New Haven and Connecticut colonies
was his father-in-law; son of Governor William Leete

narrative_blocks.header: He married the granddaughter of Governor William Leete
narrative_blocks.body: Mary Leete was the daughter of William Leete Jr. and the granddaughter of Governor William Leete — the only man to govern both New Haven Colony and the Colony of Connecticut. James Hooker, born a Farmington pastor's son, married into one of the most distinguished families in colonial Connecticut.

narrative_blocks.header: First Probate Judge — presided over the Assembly
narrative_blocks.body: He became the first Probate Judge of the Guilford district and repeatedly represented Guilford in the Colonial Assembly, serving for a number of sessions as its presiding officer. EH notes he "became a very prominent man at Guilford."

narrative_blocks.header: His only son died at 21, name extinct
narrative_blocks.body: EH notes he "had but one son who died unmarried, leaving no one to perpetuate his name." That son was William, Yale class of 1722, who died at 21.

career.notes: First judge of the Guilford probate district; also a town magistrate.
Repeatedly represented Guilford; presiding officer for several sessions.

bio.title: Honorable
bio.bio_blurb: First probate judge of the Guilford district

cross_connections.display_label: Her husband succeeded Daniel as Yale's second tutor
was her father-in-law

narrative_blocks.header: Married Yale's first B.A. graduate
narrative_blocks.body: On December 6, 1720 she married Rev. John Hart of East Guilford as his third wife. Hart had been Yale's first B.A. graduate (1703) and second tutor — succeeding her uncle Daniel Hooker, who had been Yale's first tutor.

narrative_blocks.header: Three of her six children survived to marry
narrative_blocks.body: With John Hart she had six children — four sons and two daughters. Two sons died young and infant Mary lived only three months. Sons Thomas (1723-1813) and Benjamin (1725-1804) carried the line, while daughter Sarah married three times — twice to Yale graduates.

parents.mother_research_notes: Ruth Hawkins (daughter of Anthony Hawkins of Farmington)

church_roles.notes: Bespoken as tutor on the day of his graduation. Preceded by Daniel Hooker (first tutor, 1702), who was a son of Rev. Samuel Hooker of Farmington (H00007) and brother-in-law of Rev. James Pierpont, Yale's founding patron.

Granted 25 acres by town of Guilford as settlement. "One of the first Eminence of preachers in his Day" (Rev. Thomas Ruggles Jr.). "A gentleman of eminent piety" (Rev. John Devotion). About 80 members added to church during his pastorate. In 1722, signed Rector Cutler's petition expressing doubt of Presbyterian ordination — later resolved his doubts and remained in place. President Stiles: "Mr. Hart is said to have been a man of the greatest ingenuity and learning of all the seven."

narrative_blocks.header: Yale's first B.A. graduate — alone in 1703
narrative_blocks.body: He was the first student in Yale College to be advanced to a Bachelor's Degree. He graduated alone at Saybrook on September 15, 1703 — his diploma signed by Rector Abraham Pierson, Moses Noyes, Thomas Buckingham, and Noadiah Russell.

narrative_blocks.header: Second Yale tutor — succeeded Daniel Hooker
narrative_blocks.body: He was appointed Yale's second tutor on the very day of his graduation, succeeding his Farmington townsman Daniel Hooker. He served from 1703 to 1705, with charge of the two lower classes.

narrative_blocks.header: Ordained East Guilford's first minister, 1707
narrative_blocks.body: In April 1707 he accepted the call to East Guilford (now Madison), and on November 25, 1707 he was ordained over the new thirteen-member church. Five ministers including James Pierpont and Thomas Buckingham officiated.

narrative_blocks.header: Signed the 1722 Yale Episcopal declaration
narrative_blocks.body: In September 1722 he joined Rector Cutler and five others in signing the famous declaration before Yale trustees questioning the validity of Presbyterian ordination. After conference at Yale Library on October 16, his doubts were resolved and he remained Congregational.

narrative_blocks.header: Greatest ingenuity and learning of all seven
narrative_blocks.body: President Ezra Stiles called him a man of 'the greatest ingenuity and learning' among the seven who signed the 1722 declaration. His estate at death included £35 in books — a substantial scholarly library.

cross_connections.display_label: East Guilford ordination, November 25, 1706
East Guilford ordination, November 25, 1706
East Guilford ordination, November 25, 1706
Succeeded him as Yale's second tutor in 1703

education.notes: FIRST student at Yale to receive a Bachelor's degree — graduated alone at Saybrook, September 15, 1703. His original diploma is preserved in Yale Library. The 1702 class received M.A. degrees only (honorary, for Harvard graduates); Hart's 1703 B.A. was Yale's first bachelor's degree.

bio.title: Reverend
bio.bio_blurb: Yale's first graduate; second tutor of the college

narrative_blocks.header: Eldest son of Mary Hooker, dead at eleven
narrative_blocks.body: Born January 16, 1722 in East Guilford, he was the firstborn child of Mary Hooker and Rev. John Hart. He died March 28, 1733 at age 11 — the second child Mary had lost, after baby Mary in 1724.

narrative_blocks.header: She died in infancy
narrative_blocks.body: Mary Hart was born and died in 1724, living only a few months.

burial.plot_notes: Alder Brook Cemetery (East Burial Ground), Guilford, New Haven County. FindAGrave #104157681. Inscription: "wife of Rev. Amos Fowler age 62"

narrative_blocks.header: Married three times — two Yale graduates
narrative_blocks.body: She married first Henry Hill, who died 1751. Her second husband Dr. Thomas Adams (Yale 1737) died only two years into the marriage, in 1753. Her third husband Rev. Amos Fowler (Yale 1753) outlived her — she died in 1789, he in 1800.

narrative_blocks.header: Children spanned three marriages and two centuries
narrative_blocks.body: Her firstborn was Hon. Henry Hill by her first marriage — through whom her line carried forward. With Rev. Amos Fowler she had three more children, including descendants who reached the 19th century at Guilford.

narrative_blocks.header: Granddaughter's husband owned an enslaved child
narrative_blocks.body: Her son Hon. Henry Hill's daughter Catherine Hill married Andrew Elliott of Guilford. Andrew owned an enslaved child named Betsey, baptized in 1817 at Christ Church Guilford as 'colored girl belonging to Andrew Eliott.'

narrative_blocks.header: Died single at seventeen in Madison
narrative_blocks.body: He was the youngest child of Mary Hooker and Rev. John Hart. Edward Hooker recorded simply: 'died single at Madison, September 4th, 1747, aged 17 years.' He died nine years before his mother.

narrative_blocks.header: She and her sister married two Smith brothers
narrative_blocks.body: Hannah Hooker married Thomas Smith of the New York Highlands. Her younger sister Mehitable married his brother, Rev. Dr. John Smith, joining the two families twice over.

parents.father_research_notes: Thomas Smith Sr. of Rye NY

enslaved_persons.name: Peleg
enslaved_persons.description: Enslaved man. Bequeathed in Sarah's will to daughter Anna Ruggles, then to grandson Nathaniel Ruggles Jr. if unsold at Anna's death. Probate inventory lists "Negro boy Peleg, 65 pounds."
enslaved_persons.source: Sarah Hooker Bartlett probate, Guilford, Connecticut

burial.plot_notes: Old North Cemetery, Guilford CT; grave marker effaced. SOURCE: FindAGrave.

narrative_blocks.header: Her will bequeathed an enslaved man named Peleg
narrative_blocks.body: Sarah Hooker Bartlett's will, proved after her death in 1769, left to her daughter an enslaved man named Peleg, listed among the property of her Guilford estate.

burial.plot_notes: Old North Cemetery, Guilford, New Haven County, Connecticut. FindAGrave #163942136

enslaved_persons.name: Rose
enslaved_persons.description: Enslaved woman. Estate inventory: "a Negroe woman named Rose, ninety pounds."

enslaved_persons.name: Jethro
enslaved_persons.description: Enslaved man. Estate inventory: "a Negroe man named Jethro, 210 pounds."

narrative_blocks.header: His estate inventory listed two enslaved people
narrative_blocks.body: When Captain John Bartlett died in 1747, the inventory of his Guilford estate listed two enslaved people, a woman named Rose and a man named Jethro, among his property.

cross_connections.display_label: was her father-in-law

narrative_blocks.header: Named in her mother's will at Guilford
narrative_blocks.body: Annie Bartlett married Dr. Nathaniel Ruggles and was named in her mother Sarah's will; through her line passed the enslaved man Peleg recorded in the family estate.

narrative_blocks.header: She died at Guilford at age two
narrative_blocks.body: Sybil Bartlett, born in 1723, died at Guilford in 1725, only two years old.

burial.plot_notes: Village Green Cemetery, Guilford, New Haven, CT. GPS: 41.28297, -72.68194.

bio.notes: Named for ancestor Rev. Thomas Hooker — another deliberate Hooker namesake in generation 4

narrative_blocks.header: His estate held shares in two enslaved men
narrative_blocks.body: The 1767 inventory of Hooker Bartlett's Guilford estate recorded two-thirds shares in two enslaved men, Jethro and Peleg, indicating they were jointly owned with relatives.

burial.plot_notes: Alderbrook Cemetery, Guilford, New Haven County, Connecticut

education.notes: Yale graduate — class year not confirmed

narrative_blocks.header: Yale graduate, dead at twenty-one
narrative_blocks.body: He graduated Yale around 1722 and died March 20, 1724 in Guilford at age 21, unmarried. Edward Hooker noted he 'died unmarried, leaving no one to perpetuate his name.'

narrative_blocks.header: The last Hooker of the Guilford line
narrative_blocks.body: He was the only son of Hon. James Hooker, the Probate Judge of Guilford. With his early death, the Hooker surname disappeared from Guilford within a single generation of arriving.

sources.short: Smith-Hooker Genealogy by Della Bishop, 1936, Ithaca NY

cross_connections.display_label: Mehitable Hooker Smith is the great-grandmother of Maj. Benjamin Tallmadge.

narrative_blocks.header: Twelve children with Rev. John Smith of Rye
narrative_blocks.body: She married Rev. John Smith of Rye, New York, and had twelve children between 1727 and 1750. The family lived in Westchester County during the years just before the Revolution turned that border country into a no-man's land.

narrative_blocks.header: Great-grandmother of Washington's spymaster
narrative_blocks.body: Her great-grandson Major Benjamin Tallmadge ran the Culper Ring for George Washington — the network that exposed Benedict Arnold's 1780 treason. Tallmadge was a classmate of Nathan Hale and a fellow Hooker descendant.

narrative_blocks.header: Daughter Abigail at Washington's White Plains headquarters
narrative_blocks.body: Her daughter Abigail Smith married Jacob Purdy, whose Purchase, NY house served as Washington's headquarters during the Battle of White Plains in 1776 and again in 1778. The Purdy descendants would carry the line through several Patriot–Loyalist conflicts.

narrative_blocks.header: She and her sister married two Smith brothers
narrative_blocks.body: Mehitable Hooker married Rev. Dr. John Smith of Rye. Her elder sister Hannah had married his brother Thomas, so two Hooker sisters wed two Smith brothers.

burial.plot_notes: First Presbyterian Church Cemetery, White Plains NY. SOURCE: FindAGrave.
First Presbyterian Church Cemetery, White Plains NY; later church construction extended over his grave. SOURCE: FindAGrave.

notable.notable_blurb: Physician-minister at White Plains for thirty years

cross_connections.display_label: Young friend in New York who drew him toward the ministry

career.notes: Practiced medicine alongside ministry for nearly 30 years. 'Said to have been a very successful physician... remarkably successful in cases of insanity.'
Served the White Plains church which was destroyed in the Battle of White Plains October 1776, seven years after his death.

narrative_blocks.header: Walked the Hudson with Jonathan Edwards as friend
narrative_blocks.body: He was a personal friend of Jonathan Edwards, the most important American theologian of the eighteenth century. On one occasion, walking together along the banks of the Hudson, Smith confided to Edwards: 'To be separated from my endeared friends and companions are the most bitter trials of my life.' The friendship spanned Smith's quarter-century at White Plains and Edwards's career through Northampton, Stockbridge, and Princeton.

narrative_blocks.header: Physician and Presbyterian minister for thirty years
narrative_blocks.body: He practiced medicine and ministry simultaneously at Rye and White Plains for nearly thirty years — a rare dual vocation in colonial America. Contemporaries described him as 'remarkably successful in cases of insanity.' His Yale class of 1727 trained him for ministry; the medicine he taught himself.

narrative_blocks.header: His church destroyed at Battle of White Plains
narrative_blocks.body: Seven years after his death in 1771, the Battle of White Plains was fought in front of his Presbyterian church on Oct 28, 1776 — and the building was destroyed. A second church on the same ground was later destroyed by fire. The third — built of gray stone — was eventually extended over his grave, and the original upright slab was moved further back.

narrative_blocks.header: A church was later built over his grave
narrative_blocks.body: Rev. Dr. John Smith served nearly thirty years as Presbyterian minister at Rye and White Plains. After his death the White Plains meetinghouse was rebuilt over his burial spot, and his gravestone was moved back to accommodate it.

burial.plot_notes: Jackson Cemetery, Tunkhannock, Wyoming County PA. SOURCE: FindAGrave; Miner's History of Wyoming.

notable.notable_blurb: Continental Army surgeon; Old Forge, PA namesake

cross_connections.display_label: survived the same Wyoming Massacre as a child
fought at the Battle of Wyoming with judge Matthias Hollenback
Wyoming Valley physician who soldiered with Colonel John Spalding
Wyoming Valley patriot who soldiered with General Simon Spalding

military_service.notes: 3rd Co., 1st Regt CT, May 1775, Wyoming Valley PA; marched under Gen. Schuyler to NY. 1776: 10th Continental Regt CT under Col. Parsons. 1777: commissioned Captain, 24th Regt CT (Westmoreland Regt). May 27, 1778: commissioned Surgeon. Surgeon to militia under Col. Dennison, Forty Fort 1778-1779. Participated in Battle of Wyoming. Congress 1836 special act: $2,400 to heirs for war services.

bio.notes: Middle name "Hooker" explicit tribute to ancestor Thomas Hooker

narrative_blocks.header: Continental Army surgeon at Wyoming Massacre 1778
narrative_blocks.body: He served as Surgeon in the Continental Army — 3rd Company, 1st Regiment Connecticut — and was living in the Wyoming Valley when the July 3, 1778 massacre killed approximately 300 Patriots. His first wife Sarah had died three weeks earlier.

narrative_blocks.header: Family Bible recorded wife's death before the massacre
narrative_blocks.body: His Oxford-printed Bible (1772) preserves the record of her death at 'about nine o'clock at night' on Friday, June 12, 1778. She was buried on his own land at Jacob's Plains two days later, three weeks before the Wyoming Massacre.

narrative_blocks.header: Old Forge, Pennsylvania was named for him
narrative_blocks.body: He and his son-in-law James Sutton founded an iron works in the Lackawanna Valley. Those who knew him called him 'old forge' — and the town of Old Forge, Pennsylvania was named for him.

narrative_blocks.header: Lived to ninety in Tunkhannock, Pennsylvania
narrative_blocks.body: He died July 17, 1815 in Tunkhannock, Wyoming County, Pennsylvania, age 90. He had outlived his first wife by 37 years and his second wife as well.

narrative_blocks.header: Congress paid his heirs for his war service
narrative_blocks.body: Decades after his death, Congress passed a special act in the 1830s granting his heirs $2,400 in commutation for his Revolutionary service as the sole surgeon at the Wilkes-Barre garrison in the Wyoming Valley.

narrative_blocks.header: He died at about age three
narrative_blocks.body: John Smith, born at White Plains in 1726, died in 1729, about three years old.

narrative_blocks.header: She died in infancy
narrative_blocks.body: Mary Smith was born in 1728 and died the next year, only about one year old.

burial.plot_notes: Setauket Presbyterian Church Cemetery, Setauket NY. SOURCE: research notes.

narrative_blocks.header: Her son became Washington's spymaster
narrative_blocks.body: Susannah Smith married Rev. Benjamin Tallmadge of Setauket. Their son Benjamin grew up to run the Culper spy ring for General Washington during the Revolution.

narrative_blocks.header: She died at eighteen, unmarried
narrative_blocks.body: Ann Smith, born in 1732, died in 1750 at the age of eighteen.

narrative_blocks.header: She died young, soon after marrying
narrative_blocks.body: Sarah Smith married Reuben Wright but died about 1760, only twenty-four years old.

narrative_blocks.header: He died at about age eleven
narrative_blocks.body: James Smith, born at White Plains in 1739, died in 1750, about eleven years old.

burial.plot_notes: Presbyterian Cemetery, Marlboro, Ulster County, New York. GPS: 41.60250, -73.97204.

bio.notes: Source gives surname "Purdy" by marriage — distinct from Abigail Smith Purdy

narrative_blocks.header: She lived to ninety-three
narrative_blocks.body: Abigail Smith married Capt. Jacob Purdy and lived to ninety-three, dying in 1839 — among the longest lives of her generation.

open_questions: Is wife Mary Fenn a daughter of Benjamin Fenn (X00010)? X00010 records list "Mary (bapt 1647)" among children of first marriage to Sarah Baldwin. If confirmed, this creates a Fenn cross-connection through the Leete line.

sources.notes: Mary identified as "only daughter of William and Mary (Fenn) Leete"

cross_connections.display_label: married his daughter Mary

narrative_blocks.header: Her daughter married Judge James Hooker of Guilford
narrative_blocks.body: Mary Fenn was a daughter of Deacon Benjamin Fenn of Milford. Her daughter Mary Leete married James Hooker, a grandson of Thomas Hooker and the first probate judge of the Guilford district.

narrative_blocks.header: She remarried a King William’s War captain
narrative_blocks.body: After William Leete died in 1687, Mary married Captain Stephen Bradley of Guilford, who served as an officer of dragoons and a trainband captain during King William’s War.

burial.plot_notes: Interred at the Ancient Burying Ground — same ground as Thomas Hooker.

cross_connections.display_label: sheltered the regicides at Milford, as Leete did
His granddaughter married the first Guilford Probate Judge.
governed both Massachusetts and Connecticut

notable.notable_blurb: Governor of New Haven and Connecticut colonies

narrative_blocks.header: The only man to govern both New Haven
narrative_blocks.body: He served Guilford as town clerk from 1639, rose to Governor of New Haven Colony (1661-1664), and then Governor of the Colony of Connecticut (1676-1683) — the only person ever to hold both offices. He is buried at the Ancient Burying Ground in Hartford — the same ground as Thomas Hooker.

narrative_blocks.header: He used the Sabbath and slow horses
narrative_blocks.body: When royal agents arrived demanding the regicides Goffe and Whalley, he read the King's warrant aloud in public — alerting everyone to the agents' purpose — then furnished horses so slowly that the Sabbath intervened before they could depart. By Monday the men were hidden. Roger Newton did the same in Milford; Roger Hooker did the same in Hartford — three men in the same civic network, the same year.

narrative_blocks.header: Granddaughters married into the Hooker family — two branches
narrative_blocks.body: His son William Leete Jr. fathered Mary Leete, who married Hon. James Hooker of Guilford. His son Andrew Leete fathered Mercy Leete, who married Samuel Hooker of Kensington. Two of his granddaughters independently married into the Hooker family in the same generation.

narrative_blocks.header: One of Guilford's founding settlers
narrative_blocks.body: Leete left England in 1639 to escape the persecution of Puritans he had witnessed as a court clerk. He was among the twenty-five who signed the plantation covenant at sea and helped found Guilford, becoming one of the seven pillars of its church.

narrative_blocks.header: He kept Guilford's records for over twenty years
narrative_blocks.body: Leete served as town clerk of Guilford from 1639 until 1662, alongside terms as magistrate and deputy. The long climb through colony offices carried him eventually to the governorship.

parents.father_research_notes: Rev. John Payne of Southoe, Huntingdonshire.

bio.bio_blurb: First wife of Gov. William Leete; mother of his ten children

narrative_blocks.header: Mother of all ten Leete children
narrative_blocks.body: Anna Payne, daughter of Rev. John Payne of Southoe, married William Leete in England in 1636 and crossed to Guilford with him. She bore all ten of his children and died in 1668, before he rose to the Connecticut governorship.

political_offices.title: Deputy
political_offices.jurisdiction: General Assembly of New Haven Colony

political_offices.title: Commissioner
political_offices.jurisdiction: United Colonies of New England (New Haven Colony)

sources.short: Empty Branches on the Family Tree
sources.notes: Blog post with careful analysis; corrects 1630 Mary and John arrival date to likely 1637
Chapter named in honor of Benjamin Fenn

emigration.notes: Likely arrived 1637 with a group from Buckinghamshire — NOT on the 1630 Mary and John as sometimes stated online. First appears in colonial records 1637; seven-year gap makes 1630 arrival implausible. Removed from Dorchester to Milford where he received lot #3 (2 acres 11 rods 3 poles).
emigration.discrepancy_note: Online sources give 1630 / Mary and John. Genealogical blog (Empty Branches) argues compellingly for 1637 Buckinghamshire group. 1637 preferred.

cross_connections.display_label: James Fenn's marriage to Joanna Prudden connected the Fenn-Hooker families.
James Fenn's marriage to Joanna Prudden connected the Fenn-Hooker families.
His daughter Susanna married the Hooker merchant.

notable.primary_url_label: Empty Branches on the Family Tree

narrative_blocks.header: Milford magistrate and a colonial Assistant
narrative_blocks.body: Benjamin Fenn was a deacon, magistrate, and militia officer at Milford. He represented the town in the New Haven Colony and served as an Assistant of the colony from 1665 to 1672.

narrative_blocks.header: He took lot number three at Milford
narrative_blocks.body: Born at Stewkley in Buckinghamshire, Fenn came over in the Great Migration and settled at Milford. He held land on both sides of the Atlantic for the rest of his life.

narrative_blocks.header: Two daughters married into the Hooker line
narrative_blocks.body: His daughter Martha married Samuel Newton, a grandson of Thomas Hooker; his daughter Susanna married William Hooker of Farmington. Both ties pull the Fenn family into the Hooker tree.

narrative_blocks.header: His will left each grandchild a single ewe
narrative_blocks.body: Fenn's 1672 will divided farms in Milford, New Haven, and Buckinghamshire among his sons. To every one of his grandchildren he left a single ewe sheep.

career.notes: Milford deputy 1653; magistrate 1654-1663; Commissioner of the United Colonies 1661 and 1663.

burial.plot_notes: Buried in Peter Prudden's garden, Milford Cemetery.

narrative_blocks.header: Her father died during the Atlantic crossing
narrative_blocks.body: Sarah Baldwin sailed for New England in 1638 aboard the Martin. Her father, Sylvester Baldwin, made his will on the ocean and died during the crossing, when she was about seventeen.

narrative_blocks.header: Six children, then buried in Prudden's garden
narrative_blocks.body: Sarah married Benjamin Fenn at Milford and bore six children. She died in 1663 and was buried in Peter Prudden's garden at Milford.

burialCemetery.name: Alder Brook Cemetery
burialCemetery.notes: Burial place of Dr. Nathaniel Ruggles (X00282) and Rev. Thomas Ruggles Sr. (X01929).

crossConnections.display_label: First cousins — both Leete granddaughters who married Hooker men.
